findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC for the arm-binding-identity test: specify its test file path (e.g., `tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_yaml_arms.py`), the proving command (`pytest tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_yaml_arms.py -x`), and what it asserts (the `with:` bindings for coach_inputs, the `when:` of coach_gate, and the `surviving:` of coach_notes are identical across both YAML arms).
evidence: Absence: no AC references a 'binding-identical' or 'arm symmetry' test
Absence: no pytest -k filter or test file path is named for this test
The dual-arm edit is highlighted as a risk ('exists TWICE, structurally identical') yet its guard test has no AC to declare it done or green
findings.finding: The 'unit test asserts the two arms' coach blocks remain binding-identical' is mentioned in the What section but has no AC, no test-file location, no proving command, and is not covered by any AC oracle. This structural guard — the primary mechanism preventing arm-drift — is completely unverifiable from the ACs as written.
findings.location: What section — 'A unit test asserts the two arms' coach blocks remain binding-identical'; Acceptance Criteria — no corresponding AC
scenarios: An agent implements the binding-identity test but puts it in the wrong file, or skips it entirely, with no AC to catch the omission
A future PR modifies one arm and the binding-identity test fails — but since it was never gated by an AC, the CI failure is unexpected

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC 2) with a YAML-level integration test that instantiates the `coach_gate` step (or uses the workflow runner's dry-run/evaluate-when capability) and asserts it fires when `coach_inputs.outputs.findings` is non-empty and skips when empty, covering both YAML arms.
evidence: AC 2 oracle: 'unit test (tests/unit/workflow/) stubbing a StepContext for plan_review_coach_inputs... asserting the findings output contains both' — this validates the op, not the YAML when: expression

The plan acknowledges 'the coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes/coach block exists TWICE' but the proposed binding-identity test only checks structural sameness, not that the when: fires correctly on non-empty findings
No AC contains a YAML-level integration test (e.g., running the gate step with a mock workflow runner and asserting the coach_notes step is skipped/executed based on findings emptiness)
findings.finding: The YAML `coach_gate` `when:` condition change (from `${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}` to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`) has no oracle that directly exercises the YAML branch condition itself. All AC 2 oracles test op input/output via stubbed StepContext objects, which do not exercise the YAML orchestration layer. If the `when:` expression is mis-typed or the output binding name is wrong, every op unit test stays green while the gate never fires in the actual workflow.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC 2; What section — 'change coach_gate's when: to ${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}'
scenarios: Agent mis-types `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` as `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.surfaced }}` in one arm — all unit tests pass, blocking coaching silently absent in production
The output key name in plan_review_coach_inputs is `findings` but the YAML references a different key — unit tests of the op itself still pass
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC 2: 'AND a workflow-level test using the gate evaluator asserting coach_gate fires when findings=[...] and is skipped when findings=[], for both the then-arm and else-arm YAML blocks.'

findings.finding: AC3's grep oracle (`grep -niP "advisory\\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md`) targets only the .md file, but the plan requires a second change in `src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123` (header from '## Surviving advisory findings (by id)' → '## Surviving findings (by id)'). An executor who updates the .md but forgets coach.py would see the grep pass while the coach.py header still contains 'advisory findings' — making the grep oracle incomplete for the full AC3 claim.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3 oracle: `grep -niP ...plan_review_coach.md`
findings.suggested_fix:
Change the AC3 oracle to:
```
grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py
```
returning no hits, AND the existing unit test assertion on the rendered listing header.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC-6 and the CLI section: either specify how coaching notes are correlated to their blocking finding (e.g., `finding_refs` matched to finding `id`, rendered inline) or restate the claim as 'coaching for blocking findings appears in the coaching section with guide_url' and align the oracle test accordingly.
evidence: Plan: 'rebar review-plan renders the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding the same way it does for advisories'
Plan AC-6: 'rebar review-plan output for a BLOCK verdict shows the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding.'
Live code lines 536-537: `for f in result.get('blocking', []): sys.stdout.write(...)` — renders blocking findings.
Live code lines 551-553: `for c in result.get('coaching', []): link = c.get('guide_url'); sys.stdout.write(...)` — renders coaching in a separate flat loop with no per-finding correlation.
Current advisories (lines 538-542) are also rendered as a separate block, not with coaching under each one — so 'the same way it does for advisories' describes a flat loop, not per-finding grouping.
No existing logic correlates `c.get('finding_refs')` to `f.get('id')` for per-finding rendering.
findings.finding: The plan asserts that the CLI (`_llm_commands.py`) currently renders coaching 'under each blocking finding the same way it does for advisories', but the live code renders ALL coaching entries in a single flat loop (`for c in result.get('coaching', [])`) that is not correlated or grouped under individual findings — neither for blocking nor for advisories. The plan's AC-6 says 'under each blocking finding', implying per-finding visual grouping, but neither the existing rendering logic nor the plan's change description specifies how this visual association will be implemented (e.g., match `finding_refs` to blocking finding IDs).
findings.location: AC-6 / CLI section; src/rebar/_cli/_llm_commands.py:536-553
scenarios: An agent reading AC-6 ('under each blocking finding') would expect to see each BLOCK finding printed, immediately followed by its coaching note(s) — but the current and proposed rendering just appends all coaching at the end in a flat block, which may or may not satisfy 'under each blocking finding' depending on interpretation.

The AC-6 oracle test ('rendered text contains the guide_url for a blocking finding') does not test visual grouping — it would pass even with the flat loop, making the 'under each blocking finding' claim untestable as written.

evidence: 3 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).

verify command `grep -niP "advisory\s+(findings|only)" src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md` inspects output with `grep` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to the What section a concrete specification of the `decision` map parameter: its name (e.g., `decision_map: dict[str, str] | None = None`), how it is constructed at each call site (keyed by finding `id`, values `'block'` or `'advisory'`), and the default/fallback behavior when a finding_ref is absent from the map.
evidence: Plan text: '`render_coach_notes` (src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162) gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory") stamped onto each rendered entry'
Plan text: 'the kernel `coach()` (coach.py:195...) gains a pass-through parameter for it'
Plan text: '`pass4_coach` (passes.py:842) reaches it via `review_kernel.coach(surviving, registry, pick=..., ...)` — it passes the map there'
Plan text: 'the workflow op `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:475) calls `passes.render_coach_notes` directly and passes the map itself'
No parameter name is given; no construction recipe is given for the map (is it `{fid: 'block' for f in blocking for fid in [f['id']]} | {fid: 'advisory' for f in surfaced ...}`?)
coach.py:195 current signature: `def coach(surviving, registry, *, pick, active_triggers=())` — no `decision_map` parameter exists yet
findings.finding: The `render_coach_notes` / `coach()` signature extension for the `decision` map parameter is described but not co-located with the schema: the plan does not define the parameter name, its type, its default, or how the decision map is CONSTRUCTED (i.e., from which inputs at the `plan_review_coach` op call site and at the `pass4_coach` call site) — leaving the call-site protocol ambiguous for an implementer.
findings.location: What section (decision tag / render_coach_notes / coach call paths)
scenarios: An implementer must reverse-engineer the decision map construction from the AC1 oracle ('block's entry carries decision="block"') with no authoritative recipe, risking mismatched key types (finding id vs finding_ref).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC-2) whose oracle explicitly asserts that the `coach_inputs`/`coach_gate`/`coach_notes`/`coach` binding block is structurally identical in both arms of `plan-review.yaml` (e.g., via the named parity unit test described in the What section).
evidence: "A unit test asserts the two arms' coach blocks remain binding-identical."
ABSENCE: No AC oracle references a two-arms parity test or dual-arm binding check.
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes a specific deliverable — 'A unit test asserts the two arms' coach blocks remain binding-identical' — that has no corresponding Acceptance Criterion. No AC oracle names or verifies this parity test, leaving it an orphaned deliverable that could be silently dropped.
findings.location: What section (dual-arm parity sentence) vs. Acceptance Criteria (all ACs)
scenarios: An implementer satisfies all 7 ACs but omits the parity test; a future edit makes the two arms diverge silently.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to AC-2: 'Additionally, a unit test (tests/unit/workflow/test_plan_review_yaml_arms.py or similar) asserts that the `with:` bindings of the coach_inputs, coach_gate, coach_notes, and coach steps are identical across both YAML arms (then-arm and else-arm of verify_branch).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py` to the plan's enumerated consumers and specify how coaching for blocking findings is produced (or explicitly scoped out) on the recovery path.
evidence: Plan's What section says 'A plan review whose verdict contains blocking findings emits a coaching entry… for each blocking finding' (AC1).
gate_dispatch.py:326: `verdict = orchestrator.finalize_verdict(pctx, parts, coaching=[], coverage=coverage, ...)` — coaching is hardcoded empty in the recovery path.
The plan enumerates coach_inputs, coach_gate, coach_notes, plan_review_coach, pass4_coach, sidecar, CLI, and orchestrator.py — but not _recover_plan_review_coach_failure.
ABSENCE: no mention of gate_dispatch.py anywhere in the plan's What or Scope sections.
findings.finding: `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` in `src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:287` is an unenumerated consumer of `finalize_verdict` that always passes `coaching=[]`. After this plan's widening, a coach-failure recovery on a BLOCK verdict will silently produce no coaching for blocking findings, contradicting AC1.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gate_dispatch.py:287-328 (the `_recover_plan_review_coach_failure` function)
scenarios: A review where Pass-3 decide succeeds but Pass-4 coach fails (e.g. LLM timeout on coach_notes step): the recovery path assembles a verdict from the decide partition with coaching=[], so blocking findings get no coaching note even though AC1 demands one.

The test test_plan_review_coach_failure_recovers_verdict_without_coaching (test_gate_engine_cutover.py:96) asserts coaching==[] and would pass — but the production behavior post-plan is now inconsistent with the normal-path behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:497-499) must widen its `triggers` computation to `{c for f in (blocking + surviving) for c in f.get('criteria', []) or []}`, mirroring the coach_inputs widening, so the applicable-moves filter covers both buckets.
evidence: Plan: 'plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced (it already receives both), matching coach_inputs.'
Live code line 497: `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or [])`
Live code line 498: `triggers = {c for f in surviving for c in f.get("criteria", []) or []}`

The phrase 'matching coach_inputs' implies the same widening, but the op's change spec never says 'set triggers = blocking criteria ∪ surfaced criteria' explicitly — a reader implementing this could miss the trigger widening and only add the decision map.

If a blocking finding carries criterion E6/F1 and no surfaced finding carries E6/F1, the applicable_moves() call at line 499 would still exclude moves gated on E6/F1, silently dropping coaching for that blocking finding even though it would be in the prompt.
findings.finding: The plan says `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:475) should 'derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced (it already receives both)', but the plan's change description for this op focuses on the decision map parameter and does not explicitly state that the `triggers`/`applicable` computation (currently at lines 497-499, sourced exclusively from `surfaced`) must also be widened to use `blocking + surfaced` criteria — leaving the applicable-moves filter potentially too narrow when blocking findings carry criteria not present in any surfaced finding.
findings.location: What section / plan_review_coach op description; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:497-499
scenarios: A BLOCK verdict has 1 blocking finding (E6) and 0 surfaced advisories. coach_inputs correctly includes blocking in coachable and builds triggers from E6. But plan_review_coach builds triggers only from surfaced (empty), so applicable = all always-applicable moves only — any move with applies_when=['E6'] is silently dropped from the applicable set used to filter LLM picks, causing coaching for the E6 block to be dropped at render even if the LLM picked the right move_id.

More generally, any move that applies_when includes a criterion carried only by a blocking finding will be excluded from the render's applicable registry, causing silent drop of those coaching entries.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section's `plan_review_coach` bullet, change 'derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced (it already receives both)' to make the implementation explicit: replace line 497's `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or [])` with `coachable = list(ctx.inputs.get("blocking") or []) + list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or [])` and build `triggers` from `coachable`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm the plan explicitly specifies that plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:497-499) derives the applicable-moves trigger set from the UNION of blocking + surviving (not surviving alone), and add a sentence clarifying that both the coach_inputs trigger computation AND the plan_review_coach render-site trigger computation must be updated to use the same union.
evidence: Plan: 'plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced (it already receives both), matching coach_inputs.'

workflow_ops.py:497-499: surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get('surfaced') or []); triggers = {c for f in surviving for c in f.get('criteria', []) or []}; applicable = passes.applicable_moves(moves, triggers)
render_coach_notes (coach.py:162) drops any note whose move_id is not in applicable — silence, no error
findings.finding: The plan says plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475) should 'derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced,' but the current code at lines 497–499 filters applicable_moves using only surviving (surfaced) triggers. The plan's prose is under-specified about WHERE the trigger-union change must land in the render path: if it is applied only to coach_inputs (for the LLM's applicable listing) but not to the applicable_moves call inside plan_review_coach at line 499 (which gates render_coach_notes), a LLM pick for a blocking finding whose criterion is absent from surviving will be silently dropped at render time. Currently safe because all plan-review moves are always-applicable, but it creates a latent correctness gap as applies_when usage grows.
findings.location: What section / plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475, lines 497-499)
scenarios: A blocking finding carries criterion C1; no surviving advisory carries C1; a move has applies_when=[C1]; the LLM picks that move for the blocking finding; render_coach_notes drops it silently because applicable was built from surviving-only triggers.
Future move registry entries use applies_when to scope moves to specific criteria — the latent bug activates without any further code change.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, add after the plan_review_coach bullet: 'Concretely: replace `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or []); triggers = {c for f in surviving ...}` with `coachable = list(ctx.inputs.get("blocking") or []) + list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or []); triggers = {c for f in coachable ...}` before the applicable_moves call, mirroring coach_inputs.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a formal `decision` enum (values: `'block'`, `'advisory'`) to `plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json` (under the `findings` array item schema) and to `sidecar.py`'s coaching entry definition, so the allowed vocabulary is machine-readable and schema-validated.
evidence: Plan text: 'decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory") stamped onto each rendered entry'
Plan text: 'add `decision` to that list so the tag survives persistence (additive optional field)'
plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json has `additionalProperties: false` and only defines {plan, instructions} — the plan adds `findings` array but never adds `decision`
No schema in src/rebar/schemas/ defines `decision` enum values for coaching entries
sidecar.py:445-454 shows the current slim dict has no `decision` field and no schema enforcement
findings.finding: The `decision` field's enum value vocabulary ('block' | 'advisory') is introduced by the plan but never defined in any schema — neither in the extended `plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json`, nor in `plan_review_coach_input.schema.json`, nor in the sidecar schema — so the closed output schema and the sidecar persistence have no machine-readable contract for the two allowed values.
findings.location: What section / plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json / sidecar.py:445-454
scenarios: An executor implementing the `decision` stamp has no schema contract to validate against — 'block' vs 'BLOCK' vs 'blocking' are all equally valid from the schema's perspective.
The AC1 oracle asserts `decision='block'` and `decision='advisory'` by string value, but nothing enforces the canonical casing/spelling.
findings.suggested_fix: In plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json, define the `findings` array with an item schema that includes `decision: {type: string, enum: ["block", "advisory"]}`. Mirror this in a coaching-entry sub-schema referenced by sidecar.py.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the What section that `workflow_ops.py` line 497 must change `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get('surfaced') or [])` to `coachable = list(ctx.inputs.get('blocking') or []) + list(ctx.inputs.get('surfaced') or [])` and that triggers at line 498 must be derived from `coachable`, mirroring the `coach_inputs` change.
evidence: Current code at workflow_ops.py:497: `surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get('surfaced') or [])`
Current code at workflow_ops.py:498: `triggers = {c for f in surviving for c in f.get('criteria', []) or []}`
Plan text: '`plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking` + `surfaced` (it already receives both)'
coach_inputs op at workflow_ops.py:366-374 builds triggers from `surviving` only (pre-change), plan says to change it to `coachable = blocking + surviving`
The render call at line 500 uses `applicable` derived only from `surfaced` triggers — blocking findings' criteria are excluded from trigger computation at render time
findings.finding: `plan_review_coach` (workflow_ops.py:497-499) derives applicability triggers from `surfaced` only (`surviving = list(ctx.inputs.get('surfaced') or [])`), but after the change `coach_inputs` derives triggers from `coachable = blocking + surviving`. The plan states `plan_review_coach` should 'derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced' but does not specify the exact code location (line 497-498) that must change, leaving the trigger-computation divergence between the two ops unresolved as a structural gap.
findings.location: What section / src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:496-500
scenarios: A blocking finding carries a criterion with an `applies_when` tag that would make a specific move applicable; the trigger is excluded because the render op still uses only `surfaced` triggers, so the move is not selected for the blocking finding's coaching note.

findings.finding: `plan_review_coach` op builds its applicable-moves trigger set from `surfaced` (advisory) findings only — code-confirmed at `workflow_ops.py:497-499`. The plan says the op should 'derive the trigger set from the union of `blocking` + `surfaced`', but this is described only in a one-liner without specifying which variable (`surviving` at line 497) must be replaced, leaving the exact line-level change ambiguous and easy to miss given the variable is aliased to `surfaced` three lines above where the comment about 'matching coach_inputs' already exists.
findings.location: What section: 'plan_review_coach (workflow_ops.py:475): derive the trigger set from the union of blocking + surfaced'; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/workflow_ops.py:497-499
findings.suggested_fix: Add: 'In `plan_review_coach`, replace the `surviving` trigger-set line (line 497) with `all_coachable = list(ctx.inputs.get("blocking") or []) + list(ctx.inputs.get("surfaced") or []); triggers = {c for f in all_coachable for c in f.get("criteria", []) or []}`'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state in the schema-change description whether `findings` is added to the `required` array, and if so, identify which existing tests or consumers need updating (expand-contract or note the non-breaking intent).
evidence: "The op's output schema ... is CLOSED (additionalProperties: false, properties {plan, instructions}) - add \"findings\": {...} to its properties (keeping additionalProperties: false - the closure is deliberate)."
ABSENCE: No mention of whether `findings` is added to the schema's `required` array, nor whether existing schema-validating consumers/tests must be updated.
findings.finding: The plan adds a `findings` property to a CLOSED output schema (`plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json`, `additionalProperties: false`) but never specifies whether `findings` is added to the schema's `required` array. If `findings` is marked required, any existing test or consumer that validates the old output shape (without `findings`) breaks without migration guidance; if it is intentionally optional, that must be stated explicitly.
findings.location: What section — schema change paragraph; src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json
scenarios: If `findings` is added to `required`: existing unit tests that snapshot or validate coach_inputs outputs without the new field fail immediately; callers that don't yet populate `findings` break schema validation.
If `findings` is intentionally optional (not required): old consumers continue to work, but the plan is silent on this, leaving the implementer to guess.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence: '`findings` is NOT added to the `required` array — it is an optional output — so existing schema consumers remain valid without changes. Tests that assert the full output shape should be updated to tolerate the new optional field.'

findings.finding: `render_coach_notes` (the kernel single source re-exported as `passes.render_coach_notes`) currently returns coaching entries with a fixed key set `{move_id, move_name, subject, finding_refs, coaching}` and no `decision` field. The plan says this function 'gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory")' and that 'both callers pass it', but never specifies how the function signature changes or how `decision` flows into each returned entry — leaving the mechanism by which `decision` appears in the rendered output structurally undefined.
findings.location: What section: 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map'; src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162-185
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`render_coach_notes` gains an optional `decision_map: dict[str, str] | None = None` parameter; when supplied, each emitted entry sets `decision = decision_map.get(fid)` for the first `fid` in `finding_refs` (or the first matching fid); callers build the map as `{f["id"]: ("block" if f in blocking_set else "advisory") for f in coachable}`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief ADR or update CLAUDE.md / `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` to document: (a) that blocking findings now receive coaching entries (behavioral contract change), (b) the `decision` field's valid values (`"block"` / `"advisory"`) and its semantics on coaching entries, and (c) that guide_url passthrough covers blocking findings.
evidence: Plan names no ADR/CLAUDE.md/design-doc update anywhere in the Scope or What sections

'guide_url: the passthrough attach at orchestrator.py:515-530 already keys off coaching entries' criteria - blocking entries get it for free once they exist; verify by test' — no doc update for this new behavior

'OUT: any change to move-registry contents, the subject validator, blocking/advisory decisions, thresholds, or the sidecar schema beyond the optional decision field' — scope lists exclusions but no doc update
The `decision` field flows through coach.py → workflow_ops.py → sidecar.py → CLI, a multi-component contract, with no documented spec for downstream consumers
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new behavioral contract on the review pipeline (blocking findings now receive coaching entries with a `decision` field) and a new output field contract (`decision` on coaching entries used by sidecar, CLI, and guide_url passthrough), but names no update to an ADR, CLAUDE.md, or design doc to capture this decision. The `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` is referenced as the link target for guide_url deep-links but is not listed as a documentation artifact to update.
findings.location: ## What (overall), ## Scope, Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: A future author extends the coaching pipeline and doesn't know blocking findings are now first-class coached artifacts because there's no doc capturing that architectural decision
A consumer of the sidecar payload encounters the new `decision` field with no documented spec of its valid values or semantics
The guide_url deep-link behavior for blocking findings is tested but not documented, making it invisible to contributors maintaining the criteria guide
findings.suggested_fix: Add a short section to `docs/plan-review-criteria-guide.md` (or a new `docs/adr/NNN-block-coaching.md`) covering: the decision to widen coaching to blocking findings, the `decision` field enum and its propagation path, and a note that guide_url passthrough is automatic once coaching entries exist. Reference it from the Scope section of this plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-surfacing behaviour when `plan_review_coach_inputs` fails to emit `findings`: either surface the op error explicitly in the workflow (so blocking findings are not silently uncoached) or fall back to `steps.decide.outputs.blocking` in `coach_gate` as a safety guard.
evidence: 'change `coach_gate`\'s `when:` to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` (fires when EITHER bucket is non-empty)'
No fallback or error-surfacing path is described if `coach_inputs` fails to emit `findings`
The plan notes the gate previously fired on `steps.decide.outputs.surfaced` (a decide-step output, not a derived op output), which is more resilient to op-level failure
findings.finding: If the `plan_review_coach_inputs` op raises an exception or returns without emitting `findings`, the `coach_gate` condition (`${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`) evaluates falsy and the entire coaching block is silently skipped — including for blocking findings. This is a silent regression: previously a blocking finding always surfaced in the verdict even without coaching; now a transient op failure could suppress coaching for blocks with no signal to the operator.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml coach_gate `when:` condition; What section: 'change `coach_gate`\'s `when:` to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}`'
scenarios: coach_inputs op throws due to malformed blocking list → findings output absent → gate closes → blocking findings reach agent with zero coaching → agent rewrites ACs instead of fixing oracle (exactly the recurrence the plan is trying to prevent)
Transient infrastructure error in op execution silently drops coaching for all findings
findings.suggested_fix: Keep a fallback guard: `when: ${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings || steps.decide.outputs.blocking || steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}` and add an explicit error log/metric emission in `plan_review_coach_inputs` on exception so operators see the failure rather than a quiet no-coaching result.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least a structured log line in `plan_review_coach_inputs` recording the count of blocking and surviving findings merged into `coachable`, so operators can verify and diagnose the merge path without inspecting raw LLM inputs.
evidence: No mention of logging, metrics, or tracing for the new merge logic
Observability for new failure points is a T5b checklist requirement
The plan's Scope section does not include any observability additions
findings.finding: The plan adds a new merge/derivation path (`coachable = blocking + surviving`) and a new output field (`findings`) in `plan_review_coach_inputs`, but specifies no observability (log line, metric counter, or trace attribute) for this new code path. If the merge produces wrong results (e.g., duplicates, missing entries, wrong ordering), operators have no signal.
findings.location: What section: `plan_review_coach_inputs` (workflow_ops.py:360) changes; Scope section
scenarios: Blocking findings are silently de-duplicated or dropped during list merge; coach output has fewer entries than expected; no log to diagnose
The `findings` output unexpectedly contains only surviving findings (bug in merge); blocking findings get no coaching; no metric counter to alert on

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC6 (and its CLI rendering unit test) to cover the non-happy paths: (a) blocking finding with no coaching entry generated renders a graceful fallback message, and (b) blocking finding with a missing guide_url renders without crashing.
evidence: 'blocking entries get it for free once they exist; verify by test' — optimistic assumption that guide_url is always present
No AC or plan text addresses what the CLI renders when coaching entry is absent for a blocking finding
No AC addresses missing/null guide_url for a blocking finding's criteria
findings.finding: AC6 (CLI rendering) only tests the happy path (blocking finding with a guide_url present). The plan does not specify CLI behaviour when a blocking finding has no coaching entry generated (e.g., no registry move matched) or when `guide_url` is absent — both plausible in production (new criteria not yet in the registry, or `orchestrator.py:515-530` attach silently skips).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria AC6: '`rebar review-plan` output for a BLOCK verdict shows the coaching line + guide link under each blocking finding'
scenarios: Criteria not covered by the move registry → no coaching entry → CLI silently shows no coaching under a BLOCK finding → agent has no remediation hint
guide_url lookup returns None (criteria not in guide) → CLI crashes or renders 'None' inline

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a dedicated test for the blocking-only (zero advisory/surfaced) path: assert that `coach_gate` opens and at least one coaching entry with `decision="block"` and `guide_url` is emitted when the verdict contains 1 blocking finding and 0 surviving advisory findings (cover both the kernel unit-test level in AC1 and the workflow step-context level in AC2).
evidence: AC1: 'asserting a synthetic verdict with 1 block + 1 advisory yields 2 coaching entries'
AC2: 'stubbing a StepContext for plan_review_coach_inputs with 1 blocking + 1 surfaced finding'

Why section: 'E6/F1 oracle-strictness finding family recurred 3-5 consecutive rounds on tickets fde0/0ee8/76d2 because agents reworded ACs instead of adding the demanded executable oracle' — the canonical motivating scenario is blocking-only, not blocking+advisory
The gate change is the key behavioral fix: old gate was closed on blocking-only rounds; new gate must open; no test validates this boundary.
findings.finding: The primary motivating scenario — blocking findings exist but NO advisory/surviving findings — is never directly tested. AC1 uses a 1-block + 1-advisory synthetic verdict; AC2 workflow tests also use 1-blocking + 1-surfaced. The critical coach_gate `when:` condition change (from `steps.decide.outputs.surfaced` to `steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings`) exists precisely to fire coaching when ONLY blocking findings are present, but no test covers the pure blocking-only (zero advisory) path to verify the gate opens and a coaching entry is emitted.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1, AC2; What section: coach_gate `when:` change
scenarios: A plan review where all findings are blocking and none survive as advisory (the exact recurrence pattern described in Why); the gate change silently fails to fire and coaching is still absent; the bug that motivated the whole ticket goes undetected by the test suite.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC1 to include a second assertion: a synthetic verdict with 1 block + 0 advisory yields exactly 1 coaching entry carrying decision='block' and guide_url. Extend AC2's workflow test with a second case: StepContext with 1 blocking + 0 surfaced asserts findings output is non-empty (gate would open) and the coaching note references the blocking finding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether the `decision` tag should be stamped inside the shared `render_coach_notes`/`coach()` kernel functions or at the plan-review-specific `finalize_verdict` layer (as `guide_url` is), to preserve the 'Plan-review-specific (NOT the shared render_coach_notes)' boundary documented at orchestrator.py:516.
evidence: Plan: 'render_coach_notes (src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162) gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory") stamped onto each rendered entry'
orchestrator.py:516 comment: 'Plan-review-specific (NOT the shared render_coach_notes)'
code_review/workflow_ops.py:472 and :500 call applicable_moves + render_coach_notes from the shared kernel — they would inherit a dead parameter
guide_url is attached at orchestrator.py:525-530 AFTER render_coach_notes, not inside it — the same pattern works for decision
findings.finding: The plan proposes threading a `decision_map` parameter through the SHARED kernel's `render_coach_notes` (coach.py:162) and `coach()` (coach.py:195) to stamp a `decision` field on coaching notes. The established precedent (orchestrator.py:515-530, comment: 'Plan-review-specific (NOT the shared render_coach_notes)') explicitly placed plan-review-specific enrichment OUTSIDE the shared kernel. Code-review also calls the shared kernel (code_review/workflow_ops.py:472,500) and would gain a dead optional parameter it never exercises. The `decision` tag can instead be stamped at the same plan-review-specific layer where `guide_url` is attached today (finalize_verdict / orchestrator.py:525-530), keeping the shared kernel boundary intact and eliminating the YAGNI parameter.
findings.location: What section — 'Decision tag: render_coach_notes (coach.py:162) gains an optional decision map…'; also src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:162-185,195-217; src/rebar/llm/plan_review/orchestrator.py:515-530
scenarios: Code-review callers of render_coach_notes receive an irrelevant optional parameter they never supply, widening the shared contract unnecessarily.
Future gates adopting the shared kernel inherit plan-review-specific decision semantics they may not share.
findings.suggested_fix: Apply the `decision` stamp in `finalize_verdict` (orchestrator.py) alongside the `guide_url` attach: after the loop at line 525 that sets `note['guide_url']`, also set `note['decision'] = 'block' if note's finding_refs appear in blocking else 'advisory'`. This avoids touching the shared kernel entirely, mirrors the established guide_url pattern, and leaves code-review's coach path unchanged.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` to the plan's edit targets: update its top-level `description` and the `surviving` property description to reflect the new `blocking` input and the combined `coachable` set.
evidence: plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json line 5: 'description: INPUT of `plan_review_coach_inputs`: the ticket whose plan text is assembled plus the surviving (advisory) findings the coach INSTRUCTIONS are listed from.'
plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json line 9: 'description': 'The surviving advisory findings (the decide op's `surfaced` partition)…'
Plan's What section names only the output schema for modification: 'The op's output schema src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json is CLOSED… add "findings"…'
ABSENCE: the input schema file is not mentioned in the plan's What or Scope sections.
findings.finding: The `plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json` description field still says 'surviving (advisory) findings' after the plan's changes, but the plan only names the output schema (`plan_review_coach_inputs_output.schema.json`) for update — leaving the input schema's description stale and inconsistent with the new `blocking` input.
findings.location: src/rebar/schemas/plan_review_coach_inputs_input.schema.json (the `surviving` property description and the top-level `description` field)
scenarios: A developer reading the input schema after the change sees a description that contradicts the actual behavior (the op now also accepts `blocking` and uses it to build `coachable`), causing confusion during onboarding or debugging.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `blocking` property entry to the input schema and update the top-level description to 'the ticket whose plan text is assembled plus the surviving (advisory) and blocking findings the coach INSTRUCTIONS are listed from.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a note to the Scope or What section acknowledging that renaming the header in coach_listing (coach.py:123) also affects code-review's coach prompt (code_review/workflow_ops.py:473), and confirm this is intentional (both gates benefit from the decision-neutral header) or move the rename to plan-review-specific infrastructure.
evidence: code_review/workflow_ops.py:473: instructions = review_kernel.coach_listing(surfaced, applicable)

Scope section: 'OUT: any change to move-registry contents, the subject validator, blocking/advisory decisions, thresholds, or the sidecar schema beyond the optional decision field.' — code-review not mentioned as in or out.
findings.finding: The plan's header-rename in coach.py:123 (from '## Surviving advisory findings (by id)' to '## Surviving findings (by id)') modifies a shared function — coach_listing / coach_instructions — that is also called by code_review/workflow_ops.py:473 (code_review_coach_inputs). The plan's scope section says nothing about code-review, yet this rename will change the LLM prompt text presented to the code-review coach step. Functionally neutral today (LLMs handle both wordings equally), but the blast radius is undocumented and the plan's AC3 grep oracle only checks plan_review_coach.md — it does not detect this side-effect.
findings.location: What section / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:123 + Scope section
scenarios: A code-review LLM-coach golden-output test that asserts on the exact prompt text breaks when the header changes.
The blast-radius omission causes a reviewer or implementer to miss that code-review's coach prompt changes too.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the fallback behavior when `coach_gate` fires (because `findings` is non-empty) but `applicable_moves` returns an empty set for the blocking finding's triggers — either document that existing always-applicable moves guarantee non-empty applicable, or add an explicit guard in the plan that the `coach()` empty-applicable path is acceptable and expected.
evidence: Plan text: 'change `coach_gate`\'s `when:` to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` (fires when EITHER bucket is non-empty)'
Current plan-review.yaml:184: `when: ${{ steps.decide.outputs.surfaced }}` — gates on advisory-only

coach.py:211-215: `if not applicable: return []` — if the applicable set is empty (no always-applicable moves, no trigger match from blocking criteria), the coach returns [] even though the gate fired

No fallback is described in the plan for this scenario — the plan does not address what the coaching output looks like when the gate fires but `applicable_moves` returns empty for blocking-only triggers
findings.finding: The `coach_gate` `when:` change to `${{ steps.coach_inputs.outputs.findings }}` means the coach LLM step fires for blocking-only verdicts (0 surfaced, N blocking), but the plan provides no fallback protocol for the case where `coach_notes` produces picks that reference blocking finding IDs that are absent from the `applicable` registry (because `applicable` is derived from triggers of an empty `surfaced` list when no always-applicable moves exist in a future registry configuration).
findings.location: What section (coach_gate when: change) / plan-review.yaml:184-226
scenarios: Future registry where moves have `applies_when` tags: a BLOCK verdict with 0 surfaced findings has blocking findings with criteria that match no move's `applies_when` → coach_gate fires, coach_notes LLM runs, but render returns [] coaching — the gate fired wastefully and the agent gets no coaching despite the gate opening.

findings.finding: The guide_url attachment via the production path (orchestrator.py:515-530) is not explicitly exercised for blocking findings in any AC. AC1 tests the test-only `pass4_coach` path (passes.py) which the plan explicitly notes is 'exercised only by unit tests'; AC2's workflow oracle only asserts decision='block' is present, not guide_url. The plan asserts guide_url is 'for free' and says 'verify by test' without naming which AC covers the production orchestrator path — leaving the live guide_url attachment for blocking findings potentially untested.
findings.location: AC1 oracle / AC2 oracle / What section: 'guide_url: the passthrough attach at orchestrator.py:515-530 … verify by test'
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2's `plan_review_coach` workflow test oracle to also assert `guide_url is not None` on the rendered coaching entry for the blocking finding, confirming the orchestrator passthrough works in the production code path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add one sentence of positive rationale in the Why or What section explaining why blocking findings are routed through the same LLM coach pipeline (e.g., 'routing blocking through the existing coach pipeline yields move-selected, subject-validated coaching consistent with advisory entries at zero extra LLM-call cost — the LLM already runs for any non-empty findings set').
evidence: Why section explains the problem (E6/F1 recurrence) and the cheapest remediation lever, but does not state why the unified-pipeline approach is preferable to a deterministic alternative.
ABSENCE: no sentence of the form 'the unified pipeline is chosen because...' or 'routing blocking through the same LLM step is preferred because...'
findings.finding: The plan lacks a positive rationale for why blocking findings are routed through the same LLM coach pipeline (coach_inputs → coach_notes LLM step → plan_review_coach render) rather than a simpler deterministic alternative such as post-hoc fixed-move assignment for blocking findings at the plan_review_coach render site. G6 requires the plan to state why the chosen approach fits.
findings.location: Why section / What section
scenarios: An implementer wonders whether to simplify by assigning a fixed move to every blocking finding without an LLM call — the plan gives no rationale to push back on that.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the Why section: 'The unified pipeline (blocking → coach_inputs → same LLM coach step → same render) is chosen because it produces move-selected, subject-validated coaching at no additional LLM-call cost (the call already fires when either bucket is non-empty) and ensures blocking entries carry the same decision tag + guide_url structure as advisory entries.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the `coach_listing` / `coach_instructions` function must include a decision marker in the listing line (e.g., `[BLOCK]` / `[advisory]`) so the LLM coach can tailor its move picks and subject phrases to the decision type — otherwise the decision-neutral listing defeats the stated motivation of the change.
evidence: coach.py:120: `listing = '\n'.join(f'- id={f["id"]} :: {f["finding"][:200]}' for f in surviving)` — no decision tag in the listing
Plan text: 'the hardcoded listing header ... becomes decision-neutral ("Surviving findings by id")'
Plan text: 'the kernel `coach()` gains a pass-through parameter for it' — the `decision` tag is stamped AFTER render, not before the LLM pick
The LLM picks `{move_id, subject, finding_refs}` — `finding_refs` are used to look up decision in the post-render stamp, but the LLM cannot distinguish blocking from advisory findings when picking
plan_review_coach.md:14: 'For each SURVIVING advisory finding, select...' — to be reworded, but the listing still carries no decision signal
findings.finding: The coach LLM prompt (`plan_review_coach.md`) is fed the combined `findings` listing (blocking + advisory) but the listing format in `coach_listing` / `coach_instructions` (coach.py:120) does not include the `decision` tag — so the LLM sees no indication of which findings are blocking vs advisory at pick time, creating a pink-elephant / instruction-locality gap where the downstream `decision` stamp depends on information the LLM never receives.
findings.location: What section (prompt + header reframing) / src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:120-125 / src/rebar/llm/reviewers/plan_review_coach.md
scenarios: The LLM coaching prompt receives a mixed list of blocking and advisory findings with no `[BLOCK]` vs `[advisory]` marker; the LLM may apply coaching strategies appropriate only for advisories (e.g., 'investigate this') to blocking findings that instead need 'here is what the AC must specify'.
findings.suggested_fix: Change coach.py:120's listing line to: `f'- id={f["id"]} [{decision_map.get(f["id"], "advisory")}] :: {f["finding"][:200]}'` and pass the decision_map into `coach_listing`/`coach_instructions`.

findings.finding: plan-review.yaml has TWO structurally identical coach_inputs/coach_gate blocks (agentic arm: lines 174-226; non-agentic arm: lines 252-304), but the plan describes the YAML edits once without acknowledging both arms as independent edit sites. An implementer applying the change to only one arm would leave the other arm un-widened.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml (What section, YAML edit description); lines 174-226 and 252-304
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: …' with 'plan-review.yaml: apply the following three changes identically to BOTH coach_inputs/coach_gate blocks (agentic arm, ~line 174; non-agentic arm, ~line 252): …'

findings.finding: The plan states 'both callers (the bespoke pass4_coach in passes.py and plan_review_coach) pass [the decision map to render_coach_notes]', but passes.py's `pass4_coach` (line 842) does NOT call `render_coach_notes` directly — it calls `review_kernel.coach()` (line 871), which internally calls `render_coach_notes` at coach.py:217. The internal `review_kernel.coach()` → `render_coach_notes` call is an unenumerated consumer if render_coach_notes gains a decision map parameter, and the plan's description of how `pass4_coach` 'passes it' is inaccurate.
findings.location: What section (render_coach_notes decision map); src/rebar/llm/plan_review/passes.py:871 and src/rebar/llm/review_kernel/coach.py:217

findings.finding: The YAML `plan-review.yaml` contains two fully duplicated copies of the `coach_inputs` + `coach_gate` blocks — one inside the `verify_branch` `then:` arm (code-grounded path, lines 174-226) and one inside the `else:` arm (non-code-grounded path, lines 252-304). The plan's 'What' section describes the YAML changes generically without acknowledging the duplication, so an implementor who updates only one copy silently leaves the other arm unchanged — a structural incompleteness.
findings.location: What section: 'plan-review.yaml: add `blocking:` to coach_inputs, change coach_gate when:, change coach_notes surviving:'; src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 174-226 and 252-304

findings.finding: The plan asserts 'passes.render_coach_notes gains an optional decision map (finding_ref -> "block"|"advisory"); both callers (the bespoke pass4 coach in passes.py and `plan_review_coach`) pass it, so every coaching entry carries `decision`.' But `passes.render_coach_notes` is a thin alias of `_coach.render_coach_notes` (review_kernel/coach.py:162) — a simple `(raw_notes, registry) -> list` function with no decision-map parameter. The plan never lists `review_kernel/coach.py render_coach_notes` as a change site, so the kernel function must be modified but is absent from the What section.
findings.location: What section / passes.py:75, review_kernel/coach.py:162-185

findings.finding: The plan-review.yaml workflow contains TWO structurally-identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes blocks — one inside the `code_grounded` then-arm (lines ~174-226) and an identical copy inside the else-arm (lines ~252-304). The plan's three YAML changes (add `blocking:` to coach_inputs, change coach_gate `when:`, change coach_notes `surviving:`) must be applied to BOTH arms, but the plan only describes them once without acknowledging the duplication.
findings.location: src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml, lines 174-226 (then-arm) and 252-304 (else-arm); plan section 'What' / 'plan-review.yaml' bullet
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note in the 'What / plan-review.yaml' bullet: 'Apply all three changes in BOTH arms of verify_branch (the code_grounded then-arm and else-arm are structurally identical; each contains its own coach_inputs / coach_gate / coach_notes block).'

findings.finding: The workflow YAML (plan-review.yaml) has TWO duplicate structural arms — the `code_grounded` agentic arm (then:) and the single-turn arm (else:) — each containing their own `coach_inputs`, `coach_gate`, and `coach_notes` steps. The plan's YAML change instructions name the step ids only once without calling out that both arms must be updated, creating a risk that only one arm receives the widening.
findings.location: plan-review.yaml: lines 174-211 (agentic arm) and lines 252-304 (single-turn arm); plan 'What' section: 'plan-review.yaml: add blocking: … to the coach_inputs step's with:; change coach_gate's when:; change coach_notes's surviving: binding'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the plan: 'Note: plan-review.yaml has two identical coach_inputs/coach_gate/coach_notes sequences (one inside verify_branch.then, one inside verify_branch.else); all changes must be applied to both arms.'

findings.finding: plan-review.yaml duplicates the coach_inputs + coach_gate steps verbatim in BOTH arms of the verify_branch (agentic arm lines 174-226, non-agentic arm lines 253-304). The plan says 'update the coach_gate branch when: condition' (singular) and 'widen the coach_inputs binding', but neither the What section nor the YAML edit description mentions that there are TWO copies to update. An implementer updating only the first (agentic) arm leaves the non-agentic arm with the old advisory-only behavior.
findings.location: What section (workflow path bullet) / src/rebar/llm/workflow/gates/plan-review.yaml lines 174-226 and 253-304

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
